verbatim: the terms of the law only forbid the abuse of a magistrate at session of his court
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly cites a specific law that grants magistrates a resource (protection from abuse) while they are performing their official duties in court.

verbatim: having taken their oaths to enroll only those who had not served in the field, they violated those oaths
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes an oath taken by the generals (the rule-makers in this context) which establishes that citizens who had already served in the field are to be exempt from immediate re-enrollment, favoring those who had not yet served.

verbatim: it was not reasonable that any of our citizens should be registered as public debtors out of personal enmity
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the process where a fine imposed by a magistrate and recorded by the Treasury clerks turns a citizen into a 'public debtor,' which is a loss of a resource (financial/legal standing).

verbatim: if they have power to remit, subject to rendering an account of their proceedings, they will easily be visited with the proper penalty for any wrong they have done.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The speaker argues that the Treasury clerks have the legal capacity to cancel (remit) a fine handed to them by other magistrates, identifying this as a specific administrative power/resource held by that group.
